---
title: "BAS 471 Computational In-Class Exam"
author: "STUDENT"
date: "2/24/2025"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#DO NOT CHANGE ANYTHING HERE
#If you need to re-generate the global environment or data file, highlight the exam_s25 code after the hashtag below and run that
#Remove the onlydata=TRUE if you need to recreate the .Rmd file that is your exam
library(multcompView)
#exam_s25( 693386 , onlydata=TRUE)
```


Carefully follow the instructions on each question.  You will be left-arrowing a bunch 
of objects named Quest1, Quest2, Quest3, Quest4, Quest5, Quest6.  E.g.  Quest1 <- 'laugh' or Quest5 <- 0.944 
You can hardcode the values that get left-arrowed into these objects (i.e. they don't have to be the result 
of running a command).  Respect capitalization when you are asked to create an object.  Do not round any answers. 

Make sure to SAVE this .Rmd file before uploading it to Canvas. DO NOT attempt to knit it (it can't be knitted). 
However, I recommend you do one last check of your code  
by clicking Run, Run All and making sure the prompt ends up as the usual > instead of a +. 
If it is a +, that means a parentheses, square bracket, curly bracket, etc., is not matched up!  If this happens on your exam, we 
can only give points to objects that your code successfully left-arrows. 

As a backup, also fill in your answers to the Exam on Canvas if requested. 

This exam is open book/note/internet, but no AI tools such at ChatGPT.  
This exam is strictly NO COLLABORATION.  Do not communicate or discuss verbally, electronically, in writing, etc., with anyone 
except for Petrie.  Do not share your exam or code with anyone else, including people in this or future sections. 
Violations of academic conduct are grounds for an automatic F for the course, so don't risk it and keep all the work your own! 

*************** 
 
Question 1:  aggregate

 
In the global environment you'll see a dataframe named N1 

The first column is named Status and contains our event of interest:  an entity having the level Chicken .
Use aggregate to find the probability an entity has the value Chicken for each level of Region .
Left-arrow into an object called Quest1 the NAME OF THE LEVEL that has the highest overall probability.
For example, if the name of this level was Cannon you'd do Quest1<-'Cannon', with the single quotes!
We are asking for the LEVEL, NOT the probability here, so your response needs to be a word in quotes.

```{r Q1 }
#code
```

************


Question 2:  connecting letters report

 
In the global environment you'll see a dataframe named Y2 

The first column is named Status and contains our event of interest:  an entity having the level Up 
Produce a connecting letters report that allows us find statistically significant differences in the probability that
an entity has the value Up between levels of State2 .
Left-arrow into an object called Quest2 a vector of letter(s) that you see under the Kansas level.
For example, if it had the letters bc, you'd do Quest2<-'bc'.  Make sure to include the single quotes.
NOTE:  make sure you do library(multcompView) in the R chunk so your code works! 

```{r Q2 }
#code
```

************


Question 3:  prop.test

 
In the global environment you'll see a dataframe named F3 

The first column is named Event and contains our event of interest:  an entity having the level Up 
The second column is named Day and contains the two group identities Thursday and Wednesday 
Use prop.test to produce a 95% confidence in interval for the difference in
probabilities of the event of interest between these two groups.
Note: set up prop.test so that it's calculating the difference Thursday - Wednesday 
Left-arrow into an object called Quest3 the UPPER LIMIT of this confidence interval.

```{r Q3 }
#code
```

************


Question: 4 : Monte Carlo

Sample A is generated by picking 64 integers with replacement between 1 and 6 with probabilities c(0.35,0.17,0.16,0.12,0.15,0.05).
Sample B is generated by picking 34 integers with replacement between 1 and 6 with probabilities c(0.24,0.14,0.14,0.18,0.17,0.13).

The PROBABILITY of picking a 1 is greater for sample A than for sample B ,
but the actual PROPORTION of 1s in the samples may not reveal that fact due to chance variation in the data collection process.

Via a Monte Carlo simulation, estimate the probability that Sample A will actually end up
with a LOWER proportion of 1s.
Rough sanity check: 0.12, give or take about 0.02 or so.

Develop your code so that it works for 10 trials, then obtain a final estimate of the probability with 100,000 trials.

Left-arrow your estimated probability into Quest4

```{r Qsamples }
#code
```

************


Question: 5 : Monte Carlo

The Cardinals currently have 14 wins.  The Cubs have 11.  The Brewers have 12.    The following games are yet to be played: 
 
* The Cubs and Cardinals will play each other four more times  
* The Cubs will play three games against other teams (not Cardinals/Brewers) 
* The Brewers will play six games against other teams (not Cubs/Cardinals)
 
Assume the result of each game is like a coin flip:  each team has a 50% chance of winning (regardless of who they are playing against).  After all these games have been played, what is the probability that the Cardinals, Brewers, and Cubs all have the same number of wins?  Write a Monte Carlo simulation with 100000 trials.  I was finding close to 2.5%. 
Tips: 
* For a series of games against 'other teams', you can pick at random from the vector `c(0,1)` to simulate the outcomes of the games.  You can let the number of 1s in the result be the number of wins in that series. 
* For the Cubs vs. Cardinals series, you can once again pick at random from the vector `c(0,1)` to simulate the outcomes of the games.  You could let the number of 1s in the result be the number of games won by the Cubs, and the number of 0s in the result be the number of games won by the Cardinals. 
* Let `cubs`, `brewers`, and `cardinals` be variables containing the number of wins of each team.  During each trial, they will start out at 11, 12, and 14, respectively.  Increment their values based on the number of additional wins, then use an `if` statement to determine if all three values are equal.  Hint:  the `&` allows you to check whether two logical statements are true. 
Develop your code so that it works for 10 trials, then obtain a final estimate of the probability with 100,000 trials.

Left-arrow your estimated probability into Quest5


```{r Qbaseball }
#code
```

************


The daily demand for an item is random but it ranges from 1 up to 10, with a 1 being 2.7 times more likely than a 10 (probabilities ramp down linearly). 
The inventory of the item is replenished every 25 days.  The analytics team is considering a policy where the inventory is reset to 135 upon replenishment.  Run a Monte Carlo simulation (100000 trials) to estimate the following probabilities 
a)  The probability that there is at least one 'missed demand' (i.e., total demand was 136 or more).  I was getting close to 8-9%. 
b)  The average number of missed demands (provided that at least one demand was missed).  I was getting close to 7. 
Develop your code so that it works for 10 trials, then obtain a final estimate of the probability with 100,000 trials.

Left-arrow your estimated probability into Quest6a and Quest6b

```{r Qdemands }
#code
```

************


BONUS BONUS BONUS
 
Question 7 : Monte Carlo

Each day, demand for a product comes in from at least one of three sources:

* Reliable source:  every day the demand is either 7, 8, 9, 10, 11  (equal probabilities)
* Infrequent source:  0 with probability 0.64 or 10, 11, or 12, each with probability 0.36/3.
* Rare source:  0 with probability 0.85 or either 25 or 30, each with probability 0.15/2.

Consider the total demand over 30 days. What is the probability that this total demand is at most 491 ?

Rough sanity check: 0.38 give or take 0.02 or so.

Develop your code so that it works for 10 trials, then obtain a final estimate of the probability with 100,000 trials.

Left-arrow your estimated probability into Quest7

```{r Qdemand }
#code
```

************


Congratulations, you're done!  SAVE this .Rmd file and upload it to Canvas. 

 

 

 

 

 
```{r}
#Grading Code (do not alter):
gc <-' l1/0tU63stae7qrylsld/bqgofrvoalay20sjnolcnoop00eqrtj6t7yiunttp/h1Daaouiwivnyalzxo76a8xdr4sjb/s6Mb0ai9jfbon2rnw u1Cgnoygu3nrqqsbieu0 hhwtfo8erfek87:ae l6Mhrauwttgewyrkxiwmanvl2as3o/twBw8Ad7Sn8 9j4aw7ur15229r0ot2n95kn-700fb2kq-yx29k4mt bm0lm9tx:254gq5fm:5d0go4ta. '
gc
```2025-02-24 09:45:04.685064
